author: Joel Gordan
artistdescription: An award-winning audio engineer and documentary producer, Joel Gordon has spent 36 years capturing diverse soundscapes—from Boston’s concert halls to the fields of Central Asia. A Grammy winner and creator of Art of the States, he specializes in early, new, and traditional music. His extensive field recordings preserve endangered languages and unique environmental soundscapes for Smithsonian Folkways and UNESCO hotspots.

sections.value: Joel came to Shillim as an acoustic archiver.  During his time there he created numerous recordings, across place and time, of the Shillim soundscape. His work situates itself between scientific and aesthetic modes of listening. Scientific bioacoustics often emphasizes species detection, classification, and algorithmic analysis, isolating sounds into discrete data points. In contrast, this practice values sound-in-context: the relational, environmental, and temporal dimensions that resist reduction. The human ear, with its capacity for ecological listening, perceives not just individual calls but the interplay of an entire habitat. The work thus inhabits a space between empirical study and sensory experience.

At Shillim, Joel created several recordings (approximately fifty hours in all) that range across the landscape and over the course of the day. A particularly vivid example of the dynamic sounds he recorded was the dawn chorus, as the forest animals wake up in the morning. From pre-dawn stillness to the full emergence of morning, the soundscape unfolds as a continuously evolving structure. There are moments of sudden collective intensity—like a “fader” being raised—followed by shifts in species dominance and density. Certain elements, such as the steady presence of junglefowl, provide continuity, while others enter in layered succession. The result resembles a symphony without a conductor, governed instead by distributed timing [I’m not sure what “distributed timing” means here] and mutual responsiveness of the performers.
Integral to Joel’s practice is a commitment to high-fidelity listening as an ethical gesture. The use of sensitive recording equipment in remote environments allows for the capture of spatial depth, subtle textures, and proportional balance within the ecosystem. Fidelity becomes a form of respect—an attempt to preserve not just sound, but the relationships embedded within it. By sharing these recordings, listeners are invited into immersive auditory experiences that might otherwise remain inaccessible.
Notably, some of the most compelling audio moments for Joel occurred when human sounds exist in ecological proportion: distant temple music woven into a dawn chorus, or the faint traces of a wedding carried across miles. These instances do not disrupt but integrate, revealing a balance in which human presence is one layer among many. 
Joel’s recordings from Shillim are held at the Cornell Lab of Ornithology.
